data.title: TSI: Massive 30% Dividend Raise For This Monthly Income Fund
data.description:
Overview

TCW Strategic Income Fund (TSI) operates as a closed-end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of income-producing assets. As market indices continue to experience volatility and uncertainty driven by the tech sector, TSI can offer an income-focused hedge that preserves capital. Since the fund doesn't have exposure to equities, the share price is disconnected from what's happening to the indices. The fund puts an emphasis on income generation over time, which comes with its own set of benefits and vulnerabilities.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that TSI's share price has fallen by about 2.9%. The fund has failed to hold onto the positive growth momentum of the market over the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 4.6% over the same time frame. TSI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 6.9% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. After reviewing the fund's reporting metrics, I believe that TSI is capable of keeping its distributions consistent over the next 12 months.

While TSI has proven to be a great income position, there are still some risks that should be considered. For instance, the exposure to debt securities introduces some interest rate sensitivity over a longer holding period, which means that near-term performance can remain limited if interest rates stay elevated. Additionally, the focus on income generation can result in severe underperformance, so this fund only makes sense for investors that seek safety over total return. So, let's start by taking a look at the strategy that TSI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, TSI has total managed assets of $247.6M that are spread across 682 different holdings. The fund has an objective of providing attractive total returns with an emphasis on high current income. In order to achieve this goal, the fund invests in a blend of different income-producing securities across different industries. So instead of investing in equities and relying on the earnings growth of its positions, the fund's focus on debt securities means that TSI is betting on a borrower's ability to repay its loans.

Looking at the portfolio breakdown, we can see that TSI is most heavily allocated towards corporate securities at 26.1% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities and non-agency mortgage-backed securities, which accounts for 23.4% and 23.7% of assets, respectively. Since the combination of these three asset types makes up more than 73% of total assets, investors should anticipate that these will have the greatest impact on TSI's daily price movement and total return potential.

While it isn't specifically highlighted in the fund's documents, I would assume that a significant portion of TSI's assets are structured on a floating rate basis. Therefore, TSI may be able to benefit from this high interest rate environment and collect more income from its positions. A high interest rate directly translates to higher interest income that can be collected from borrowers. However, a high cost of debt can also suppress the valuation of these securities.

To offset the risks associated with a higher interest rate environment, the majority of TSI's assets are allocated to securities with strong credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating above BBB are classified as investment grade and have the lowest risk of being impacted by defaults. The fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 69.7%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 37.7%
  • Not Rated: 2.7%

Valuation & Performance Risk

Since TSI is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, TSI now trades at a discount to NAV of 7.62%. For reference, TSI has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.28% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund now trades at one of the most attractive price-to-NAV discount levels over the last five years. While this can be an indication that it can be a solid time to add, I wanted to review the most updated report that is available to shareholders.

The semi-annual report for 2025 is currently the most updated report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.15 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.10 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that TSI is aligned to generate net investment income that is on par with prior years. This is an indication that the fund's portfolio strategy is efficient at generating income through different market cycles. However, the prior years also reveal that net investment income levels do not always outpace the distributions being paid. Therefore, TSI is sometimes reliant on net realized gains to bridge that gap in earnings.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary since they are directly influenced by the momentum of the market. For instance, TSI experienced a large net realized loss of $0.69 per share in 2022 and still hasn't generated net realized gains in any of the following years that have offset this prior loss. So periods of large declines can drastically impact the NAV growth that is experienced over time. As it currently stands, TSI's NAV still sits below its pre-pandemic level. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $5.85 per share
  • 2021: $5.69 per share
  • 2022: $4.94 per share
  • 2023: $5.05 per share
  • 2024: $5.03 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $5.15 per share

Since TSI has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions and pays out most of its earnings as distributions, this results in limited price growth over a longer holding period. For instance, TSI's share price has declined by 7.8% over the last 10-year period, despite market indices racing to new all-time highs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 72.1% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns may come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

As part of the fund's operating strategy, it may also deploy leverage up to 33% of total assets in an effort to amplify its earnings. This is an aggressive use of leverage during this high interest rate environment, which can actually increase the overall risk profile. Leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated. In a higher interest rate environment, the cost of debt can actually negatively impact TSI's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities. This will actively suppress the share price until interest rates trend lower and improve the fund's operating spread.

Although the Fed has initiated several 25 basis point cuts throughout 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. As we can see below, TSI's share price remains far below its pre-pandemic range. Therefore, it may actually be a great time for patient investors to accumulate at these levels if you are optimistic that lower interest rates will eventually serve as a catalyst for TSI's share price. A lower cost of debt can attract new borrowers in the market, which management can capitalize on and grow their base assets over time.

Lastly, investors should limit their expectation on the fund's ability to provide resilience through severe downturns. If the fund sees large net realized losses, TSI's share price will also decline alongside market indices. TSI may find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earned, which will cause the NAV to erode. While distributions can help offset losses, the actual share price may still see larger losses than a traditional index-tracking ETF, such as the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). As we can see below, SPY saw a total loss of 18.17% through 2022's decline. While TSI's total return was slightly lower at 14.31%, the share price still suffered from a greater decline through the year.

Dividend Raise Is Supported

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0283 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.9%. This latest declared distribution also represents a massive 30.4% raise, which can be an indication of management's confidence about future earnings. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.3396 per share. Referring to the same semi-annual report, the fund has an inconsistent coverage history, but I still believe that TSI will be able to sustain payouts over the next twelve months. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.40 per share
  • 2021: $0.21 per share
  • 2022: ($0.43) per share
  • 2023: $0.47 per share
  • 2024: $0.37 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.25 per share

As we can see, the fund has no issues generating earnings that can outpace the estimated annual payout amount. Therefore, TSI can be a great choice for investors that seek a source of reliable monthly income. Assuming that TSI is capable of capturing a similar earnings amount over the remainder of the 2025 period, the fund should have produced enough excess to capture some NAV growth and may lead to a supplemental distribution in the future.

Estimated 2025 earnings of ~$0.50 per share / $0.3396 annual payout = 1.47 years of coverage.

However, it's important for investors to consider the fact that all distributions received from TSI may not offer tax efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the last payout was classified as net investment income, which is considered ordinary dividends and has the greatest tax consequence for investors. Therefore, a position in TSI would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that TSI is a buy at its current price-to-NAV valuation. After reviewing the fund's reporting, I believe that TSI is aligned to continue generating earnings that can support the distributions being paid. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, I believe that there's a good chance TSI's share price will eventually recover back to its pre-pandemic levels. A lower cost of debt may be a growth catalyst since it will improve operating spreads and reduce the burden of leverage. Historically, the fund has done a great job at preserving capital over a long period of time, and I believe that patient investors will benefit from accumulation.

data.title: Landstar System: The Selloff Is Valid, But It's Still Fully Priced
data.description:
It has only been two months since my previous coverage of Landstar System, Inc. (LSTR). And yet, we have already seen notable changes, which justifies my downgrade from strong buy to buy. True to the title of my previous article, LSTR really did reach my target price of $155.69 and even exceeded it before selling pressures took over and dragged it down to $142.69. I can’t blame the market due to the existing structural industry challenges, macroeconomic volatility, and even AI. Despite this, LSTR’s fundamentals and operational efficiency ensure its resilience and durability. Nonetheless, caution has become more logical as valuation now shows limited upside. This is in line with the technicals that show early bearish tendencies, although buying opportunities are present.

LSTR Q4 2025: Challenges Persist

Even now, macroeconomic headwinds are still visible amid the rising cost pressures and softer demand. Freight and logistics are among the first to experience the blow due to their changing rates and volumes/loads. Landstar System, Inc. is no exception as its lackluster growth continues. Despite this, its asset-light and well-diversified business model provides it with some insulation. But of course, this was not enough to compensate for the weaker market dynamics.

During Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $1.17B, down by 2.9% YoY from $1.21B. Up to this point, LSTR still does not indicate a strong rebound anytime soon. If you remember, the YoY revenue increase in my previous coverage was 0.7%. Weaker demand and pricing power in platform truckload, ocean cargo, and other truck carriers were evident. Stubborn inflation and tariff woes still had an effect on the market, so it's not surprising that LSTR was also affected despite some nuances in its business model. On a lighter note, its LTL rate and loads increased, suggesting that it continued to benefit from the LTL market undercapacity. Rail intermodal also had some strengths. More interestingly, its revenues and loads via BCO independent contractors still increased.

Likewise, its operating costs and expenses decreased, which shows its efforts to improve efficiency. It can also imply prudent fleet management, supported by the lower fuel prices. This is evident in its purchased transportation and commissions. The only problem was the huge increase in insurance and claims, which remained rampant in the industry. Overall, its actual performance weakened, but cost-management strategies helped it maintain positive profits.

Challenges

Stubborn inflation is still a big risk factor for LSTR. At 2.7%, this is still close to the 14-month high of 3.0%. Prices are still increasing, and so are cost pressures. LSTR may also have to raise employee wages and driver insurance to help them cope with the rising prices. This may be exacerbated by new tariffs since they raise the domestic production costs, raising prices further. With that, purchasing power and pricing flexibility across industries may weaken. The volume of goods produced and transported may also be affected. That is why the number of loads in many of its transportation platforms decreased.

AI fear trade also appears to be sweeping the market. Concerns about artificial intelligence potentially disrupting the market are spreading. Although those are still speculative, the reaction is huge and sharp. And I think that this was due to the idea that investors are cutting old economy and shifting to pure AI or tech plays. With that, trucking companies that are investing in AI and other modern technologies may support their upside. In response, LSTR may also have to scale up its investment here since AI is expected to drive efficiency through better route optimization and timely road traffic updates.

Mitigants

Despite these challenges, LSTR still has some strengths to insulate its business. Its business model is already an insulation. I’ve already explained this one repeatedly. Its asset-light and well-diversified business model allows it to spread risks, manage its cost sensitivity, and lower capital intensity. And because BCOs are the ones that provide drivers, their exposure to driver shortages and higher insurance risks is not as high as many market players.

Liquidity is another important strength of the company. Its cash and cash equivalents are still adequate at $396.7M. If we include short-term investments, the total will be $452.2M. This is equivalent to 28% of the total assets, which makes it very liquid. In addition, this is more than enough to cover the total debt. With that, it still has a huge capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity. More importantly, it may continue paying regular and special dividends.

With respect to the LTL market, LSTR still has some opportunities to take advantage of. The market still experiences undercapacity due to driver shortages and the bankruptcy of many players in 2023-2025. Note that this wasn’t limited to small players. Names like Dolche Truckload, Daniel Trucking International, Inc., and Elite Carriers were on the list. In fact, there were approximately 10,000 carriers that closed their doors just in H1 2024. With that, the stronger ones who remain in the market capture more demand. And because e-commerce and smaller packages are still resilient, the LTL market benefits from these trends. It’s not surprising that the LTL PPI is soaring to its 10-year high of 268.92. In line with that, LSTR sets higher LTL rates.

Lastly, it may benefit from the policy easing cycle. This can help businesses and consumers cope with inflation and tariffs. Because of lower borrowing costs, production and consumption will stay resilient, and so will freight and logistics. Additionally, it may help LSTR manage liquidity and profitability better, since lower interest rates can also lower the repricing of its variable-rate borrowings.

Valuation

The stock price drop of LSTR is valid if you relate it to the TP, or target price, in my previous analysis. It has already exceeded my TP. As an investor here, I decided to lighten my share here. Of course, its softer performance is another factor. And as I look into price ratios, I also understand that LSTR is still fully priced.

Using P/E, the stock is now trading at 43.01x. This is so high if you ask me. It is also higher than the five-year average of 25.51. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $84.43. Of course, this is not conclusive due to several factors. First, the market was highly volatile during the pandemic years of 2020-2022. In 2023-2024, the freight recession began. Since 2025, the market has been attempting to normalize, but weakness is still evident. But more or less, LSTR is fully priced. Meanwhile, P/S shows that the stock is still reasonable. However, it also appears fully priced since the TP is $142.78.

Lastly, P/B suggests a TP of $149.47. If you look at the trend of BVPS in 2021-2025, you can say that the price has been most reactive and consistent with it. The stock increased when BVPS increased, and vice versa. Nonetheless, the gap between the TP and the current price is still narrow. So, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

LSTR shows bearish tendencies due to the huge selloff last Thursday. It broke below the first two key support levels. Now, it’s trading below the 50-day SMA, which suggests that the current momentum is weaker than the average in the past 50 days.

Despite this, the possibility of a retraction and a consolidation should not be ignored since it still moves above the 200-day SMA. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, so market sentiments are still on the positive side.

Its MACD and RSI support this. The MACD line is decreasing, while the histogram is within negative territory, which shows that the selloff exceeded buying volume. However, this has already led to overselling, which may prompt investors to take advantage of the huge dip

In the end, these contrasting indicators warrant some caution as we weigh their impact with fundamentals and valuation.

Conclusion

Landstar System, Inc. remains resilient despite the big headwinds it faces. Its strategic business model and liquidity provide some insulation as it takes advantage of some LTL market opportunities. However, valuation warrants some caution as the stock appears fully priced. Technicals adhere to it as bearish tendencies remain evident, although the overselling may open new entry opportunities. I’m downgrading my rating from buy to hold.

data.title: Tenaris: 'The Coiling Spring' Of The Energy Patch
data.description:
Tenaris: A Proxy for Rigs

Tenaris (TS, TNRSF) is probably one of the most underfollowed companies that plays a global role in a key industry. In fact, as I already explained when I initiated my coverage of the company, it is one of the leading manufacturers of steel pipes for the oil and gas industry. It specializes in oil country tubular goods (OCTG), and it is also at the forefront of offshore and onshore pipeline manufacturing technology. It is truly spread worldwide, with manufacturing, R&D, and service centers on every continent. I

It has a well-diversified customer base since it serves most of the large oil companies. At first glance, we could think that Tenaris is just a proxy for steel prices. But this is far from the truth since Tenaris' products belong to the premium category and come together with the company's technological know-how, its logistics support, and its execution. Being exposed to upstream companies, Tenaris is cyclical. Simply said, when oil and gas operators drill, Tenaris sells more pipes, and its margins improve. When drilling decelerates, so do Tenaris' sales, and its margins compress. So, we could say that Tenaris is perhaps a proxy for active rigs. As we can see, the latest analysis that the EIA released shows that the most recent rig peak was in December 2022. Right now, we are seeing mildly declining activity, which makes some analysts believe that we could be close to a trough.

At the same time, the latest U.S. crude oil production estimates that we will continue seeing output rates near the 2025 record of 13.6 million b/d.

So, even though rigs are fewer, oil production is near record levels because the industry has achieved outstanding improvements when it comes to drilling efficiency. Lateral wells, for example, are a big game-changer because they stem horizontally from one vertically drilled well. In the Permian, for example, rigs declined by 29% between 2022 and 2025, while oil production actually increased by 18%.

Since Tenaris uses steel, it is exposed to steel tariffs. However, its manufacturing footprint has meaningful U.S. exposure, and this surely helps. Here, the World Steel Association projects that in 2026 there will be a "modest rebound of 1.3%" in global steel demand, driven by a rebound in the U.S., Europe, and the Middle East. Steel is clearly an input that Tenaris uses. Since Tenaris is vertically integrated, that is, it can produce its own steel that it then turns into pipes, when steel prices (hot-rolled sheet/strip) increase, Tenaris has an advantage over the competitors that need to buy steel on the open market. This is also true with steel scrap, which Tenaris also produces internally. As we can see, scrap prices have come down from the 2022 peak, but they are still moderately elevated. As we will see in a moment, Tenaris has been able to keep its EBITDA margin high. In addition, since Tenaris produces steel also within the U.S., it is protected from import tariffs and from competitive importers from China and other countries.

Tenaris is also exposed to Brent/WTI prices because they influence the budgets of Tenaris' customers. Usually, when WTI stays between $65 and $75 per barrel, Tenaris is able to report EBITDA margins of 24%. If oil prices move down below the breakeven point (for the Permian, it is between $45 and $55), there could be another decline in rig count, which could pressure Tenaris' business and reduce its margins to around 20%, which, in any case, is still very healthy. Conversely, if oil moves above $85/barrel, Tenaris will see growing volumes and will benefit from operating leverage.

Tenaris' Main Financials

We will soon read Tenaris' FY25 report. However, Tenaris said during the Q3 earnings call that it expects stable sales and an EBITDA margin between 20% and 25%. This means that we expect revenues of $2.85B, which means that it could end the fiscal year with revenues between $11.8B and $12B (the consensus is $11.9B).

The company is known for keeping its SG&A always under control at around 14% of revenues. Interest expense is almost nonexistent, being less than $10M.

After all, Tenaris' balance sheet is nothing but superb. It holds $3B in cash and cash equivalents, and its total debt is $478M. Tenaris issues debt, but it has been repaying more than it issues (TTM debt issuance: $917M, TTM debt repayment: $1.11B). Its inventory is always stable around $3.5B, which is quite impressive for an industrial company that is exposed to some cyclicality. As a result of this solidity, Tenaris has been able to buy back shares aggressively: since late 2023, the share count is down by 11%, and this doesn't yet reflect what happened in Q4, when share repurchases continued. On top of this, Tenaris pays a nice 3.6% dividend, paid semiannually in May and November.

Tenaris is able to generate around FCF between $1.7B and $2B per year, with strong years that can achieve $2.2B. In a situation where interest expense is minuscule and capex is steady at around $650M per year, we have a perfect setup to jump onboard during the softer part of the cycle to expect FCF growth and larger shareholder returns when Tenaris' cash position explodes.

Since Tenaris has almost no debt, its key financial metric remains EBITDA because it directly influences the trajectory of its EPS. This means that we need to focus on its volumes. While we expect North America to remain more or less flat, as rig activity is softening, international volumes are usually linked to long-cycle projects, which are more difficult to turn on and off as fast as it can be done with shale. Currently, big offshore projects in Guyana, Brazil, and the Middle East could compensate for U.S. rig activity deceleration. Since Tenaris has around 40% of its business in North America but the remaining 60% around the world, we have a balanced mix, which makes me think that a little bit of growth will happen in 2026. Surely, we won't see its EBITDA margin above 25%, which would be an extremely bullish target for Tenaris. But neither will we see it below 18%, which would probably trigger a rerating.

Tenaris' Valuation

Well, even though Tenaris now trades at $48, which is an all-time high, its fwd PE is extremely interesting: 13.6. Its fwd EV/EBITDA is 7.6, while its P/FCF is a bit more expensive and stands at 13.6. The real drag to this interesting setup is that Tenaris' growth forecast for 2026 is not great, and many analysts believe that we will still see some margin compression and an EBITDA decline.

However, these numbers will soon change once Tenaris reports FY25 and initiates its 2026 guidance. In fact, they are currently based on the 2025 estimates. But once the fwd looking metrics roll over to 2026, we will be talking about a company that could report $12.2B in revenues (the consensus is $12B) and $2.1B in net income. In fact, from what we are seeing, 2026 is not going to be a bad year for Tenaris, and the company might report some growth and even some margin expansion. If this is the case, let's imagine what the PEG ratio could be. The fwd PE is 13.6, and 2026 EPS could grow 3% YoY. This would lead to a 4.5 PEG ratio, which is expensive. But if margins do expand and we also factor in the positive effect of the buybacks ($1.2B, which is equal to 5% of the current market cap), we could expect the company to end the year with 6% or 7% EPS growth, which would compress the PEG ratio to around 2. Still a bit expensive, but we would need to consider that Tenaris is going through a trough and a new peak will come after 2026.

However, for cyclical companies, the PEG ratio is not the best metric because growth rates swing. This is why I want to add the EV/EBITDA perspective, split into three scenarios. We always use the forecast of revenues near $12.2B, then we use three different EBITDA margins, which give three different values for EBITDA. A very cheap multiple would be below 6. In this case, we don't see it, even in the bull case scenario. So, we could say that the stock is fairly priced right now. By the way, a fair price means a fair deal, which is different from an expensive deal.

Scenario Revenue 26E EBITDA Margin EBITDA 26E EV/EBITDA
Bear $12.2 B 19.0% $2.32 B 9.2x
Base $12.2 B 21.5% $2.62 B 8.2x
Bull $12.2 B 24.0% $2.93 B 7.3x

However, I think the bull case for Tenaris is real because we are in a phase of the cycle where the company is preparing for the next peak. We could say it is a coiling spring. This is why I think the stock should be on our watchlist, especially if we are understanding that a company sitting at the crossroads of industrials and energy will probably see favorable tailwinds for several years. We could wait for a pullback, which could give us a 5% or 10% extra margin of safety. But, even considering where the stock is at right now, we are still in value territory. Tenaris continues to be a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Commodities: Further U.S.-Iran Talks Set For This Week
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy, and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy - Some OPEC+ members keen to increase output

While oil prices edged higher on Friday, helped by a lower-than-expected US CPI print, ICE Brent still settled down week-on-week. However, there is still a large risk premium priced into the market given the uncertainty over how the situation between the US and Iran evolves. Comments from President Trump at the end of last week, saying that regime change would be best for Iran, will likely not ease concerns.

These comments come ahead of further US/Iran talks scheduled in Geneva for Tuesday. Meanwhile, the US will also be leading the Russia/Ukraine talks starting the same day in Geneva. A tone which is more de-escalatory should see the market start pricing in a smaller risk premium, which would allow more bearish oil fundamentals to take centre stage, driving oil prices lower.

There is growing noise around what OPEC+ may decide for April production levels when they meet on March 1. The group had suspended supply increases in the first quarter of this year due to seasonality. However, with our balance sheet continuing to show a large surplus in the second quarter, there is no need for the group to bring additional supply onto the market from April. Despite this, some OPEC+ members reportedly believe that the market can cope with additional supply increases. In our balance sheet, we are not assuming further increases from the group, so clearly, if further supply is brought onto the market, it will only lead to our surplus expectations growing.

There were only minor changes in speculative positioning over the week in ICE Brent. Speculators increased their net long by 2,813 lots to 281,062 lots as of last Tuesday. Lingering supply uncertainty when it comes to Iran, as well as Russian oil flows, has left speculators reluctant to sell the market.

Metals - US aluminium tariff rollback talk brings little relief

Reports that the US administration is considering rolling back part of its aluminium tariff regime did little to shift market sentiment. Any changes under discussion appear focused on derivative products rather than primary aluminium, meaning the core 50% levy stays in place. As a result, none of the structural pressures in the US market ease - domestic smelting capacity remains limited, dependence on Canadian supply unchanged, and the Midwest premium elevated after last year’s tariff hike.

Tariffs have already reshaped US trade flows, pushing primary metals away from the US and boosting scrap inflows, while some Canadian metal has been diverted to Europe. Even if derivative levies are eased, the current market dynamics won’t change much. And with exchange inventories effectively at zero, ongoing destocking and reports of large Q2 spot inquiries emerging, the physical market remains severely constrained.

Any rollback limited to derivatives wouldn’t feed through to LME pricing and would have only a modest impact on the Midwest premium. The broader picture is unchanged - global aluminium supply remains tight, inventories thin, speculative positioning elevated, and regional markets increasingly fragmented by policy risk.

Agriculture - Wheat falls on bumper output estimates

CBOT wheat prices came under pressure last week, with prices settling 1.8% lower on Friday, after India announced it would allow controlled wheat exports. There are reports that India, the second-largest wheat producer, approved exports of 2.5mt of wheat along with an additional 500kt of wheat products (wheat flour and semolina) following more than three years of export curbs. Additionally, improved crop estimates from Russia, Australia and France for the season have added further pressure on prices.

Recent numbers from France’s Agriculture Ministry show that 91% of the soft wheat crop is rated in good to excellent condition as of February 9, above 89% in the previous week and 73% in the same period last year. In contrast, 87% of durum wheat is rated in good to excellent condition, below the 93% seen the previous week but above 84% at the same stage last year.

The latest CFTC data shows that money managers decreased their net short in CBOT corn by 20,567 lots to 48,210 lots as of February 10. In contrast, speculators increased their net short in wheat by 3,900 lots to 85,655 lots. For soybeans, the net speculative long rose by 94,316 lots to 123,148 lots. This move was driven by both fresh longs entering the market and short covering amid expectations that China will boost purchases of US soybeans this season.

Positioning data in softs was more bearish. Speculators sold 24,415 lots of No.11 raw sugar to leave them with a net short of 211,789 lots - the largest short position held since 2019 - on the back of expectations of a large sugar surplus in the 2025/26 season. Meanwhile, despite the significant downward pressure we continue to see in cocoa prices, with London cocoa down almost 16% last week, the net short in London cocoa has not moved much over the last reporting week - speculators sold just 489 lots, leaving them with a net short of 24,468 lots.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: abrdn Focused U.S. Small Cap Active ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The Fund (AFSC) gained (gross of fees) but underperformed the Russell 2000 Index.

At the stock level, Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) detracted from performance. The company’s shares dropped sharply after its hypercortisolism drug failed to secure FDA approval, raising concerns about pipeline strength and future growth prospects. Stride (LRN) was also unfavourable. Its shares fell as technology implementation issues fuelled customer churn and pressured full-year projections. Meanwhile, e.l.f. Beauty’s (ELF) shares fell after the company reported a decline in organic sales and guided for slower growth, reflecting tough comparisons against last year’s significant distribution gains.

Conversely, Alphatec’s (ATEC) shares rose and contributed to performance after a strong earnings report highlighted accelerating sales, market share gains in the spine segment and improved profitability. Aritzia (ATZAF) was also positive. Its shares gained as demand accelerated through the quarter, with new store openings and a mobile application launch reinforcing prospects for sustained growth. Meanwhile, Viavi Solutions’ (VIAV) shares rose after the company reported strong third-quarter results and successfully completed the acquisition of a high-speed Ethernet testing business, expanding its exposure to data-centre end markets.

To obtain performance through the most recent month end, call 1-844-383-7289.

The abrdn Focused U.S. Small Cap Active ETF acquired the assets and liabilities of the predecessor mutual fund, the abrdn Focused U.S. Small Cap Equity Fund, on February 18, 2025. As a result of the transaction, the Fund adopted the accounting and performance history of its predecessor mutual fund. Performance results including NAV and Market Price returns shown prior to February 18, 2025 reflect the performance of the Institutional Class shares of the predecessor mutual fund.

Performance information for periods prior to February 29, 2024 does not reflect the current investment strategy. Returns prior to December 3, 2021 reflect a predecessor fund’s performance. Please consult the Fund’s prospectus for more detail. Returns prior to May 22, 2013 reflect the performance of a prior, unaffiliated adviser.

Expense ratios (%)

Gross/Net Expense Ratio

2.12/0.65

Market review

US small caps rose over the quarter, but lagged the broader US equities asset class.

The US Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered two 25-basis-point cuts during the quarter, first in October and again in December, lowering the federal funds target range to 3.50%–3.75% as it maintained its data-dependent approach. At the December meeting, the Fed also unexpectedly announced that it would expand its balance sheet by purchasing short-term Treasuries to support market liquidity. Throughout the period, policymakers continued to emphasise the importance of returning inflation, as measured by the core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index, to 2% while placing greater weight on signs of labour market softening. The decisions occurred amid some internal dissent and, earlier in the quarter, political pressure for faster easing. Even so, the Fed’s ‘dot plot’ – the chart showing each policymakers’ projected path for interest rates – indicated that the central bank expects to cut rates only once in 2026, whereas investors are pricing in another two reductions.

A partial US government shutdown began at the start of October after lawmakers failed to reach a funding agreement and persisted for 43 days, the longest in US history, before a temporary deal was approved. The prolonged stand-off furloughed hundreds of thousands of federal workers, disrupted key government services and delayed the release of important economic data, weighing on near-term growth and investor confidence. Although the shutdown ended in November, its effects continued into December as several releases were postponed or cancelled, limiting visibility on underlying economic momentum.

The third-quarter earnings season concluded with results broadly exceeding expectations.

Activity

We initiated several positions. Alphatec Holdings is an innovator in spinal procedures. We believe its unique solutions address limitations in traditional approaches and position the company for sustained market share growth.

AZZ (AZZ) is a leading provider of metal coating solutions for construction, infrastructure and industrial end markets. We believe AZZ is well positioned to benefit from secular demand tied to grid modernisation, domestic manufacturing reshoring and accelerating data-centre development.

CarGurus (CARG) is a multinational automotive platform that connects consumers and dealers. With a leading digital marketplace, expanding product suite and increasing dealer engagement, we believe CarGurus is well positioned to deepen customer relationships and drive sustained growth.

Everus Construction Group (ECG) is a leading provider of specialised engineering and construction contracting services and the company stands to benefit from large-scale domestic infrastructure investments.

Helios Technologies (HLIO) is an industrial technology company specialising in highly engineered motion control solutions. We believe the company’s improved execution could continue to drive margin expansion, well-positioning it to benefit from a cyclical recovery across major industrial end-markets.

Kiniksa Pharmaceuticals (KNSA) is a biotechnology company developing innovative treatments for rare diseases. The company’s leading therapy for autoinflammatory conditions continues to see strong growth, with encouraging progress within its pipeline.

OneSpaWorld (OSW) is the leading provider of health and wellness services to cruise operators. We believe the company’s strong labour management, logistics capabilities and continuous operational improvement can drive sustained growth.

Supernus Pharmaceuticals (SUPN) is a specialty pharmaceutical company focused on the treatment of diseases in the central nervous system. We believe the company could benefit from its differentiated products in attention-deficit/ hyperactivity disorder and Parkinson’s disease.

Conversely, we sold our positions in Integer (ITGR), La-Z-Boy (LZB), Merit Medical Systems (MMSI), NMI Holdings (NMIH), REV Group (REVG), SM Energy (SM), Stride, WSFS Financial (WSFS) and Vertex.

Outlook & strategy

Despite the recent market turbulence, we remain optimistic about the long-term outlook for domestic small-cap stocks. Secular trends such as increased infrastructure investment, accelerating technological innovation and the reshoring of supply chains present compelling opportunities for smaller companies. At the same time, the strategy’s emphasis on higher-quality businesses offers a level of resilience that helps mitigate macroeconomic headwinds. By combining structural tailwinds with a disciplined focus on quality, we are confident in the strategy’s ability to identify companies capable of navigating volatile markets and growing sustainably through economic cycles.

Important Information

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, fees, charges, and expenses of an ETF before investing. The summary and full prospectuses contain this and other information about the ETF and should be read carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus for the ETF, contact us at 1-844-383-7289 or click here.

ETF shares are bought and sold at market price (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. As a result, an investor may pay more than net asset value when buying and receive less than net asset value when selling. In addition, brokerage commissions will reduce returns. Fund shares are not individually redeemable directly with the Fund but blocks of shares may be acquired from the Fund and tendered for redemption to the Fund by certain institutional investors in Creation Units.

The value of a security may decline for reasons directly related to the issuer, such as management performance, financial leverage and reduced demand for the issuer’s goods or services. The stock or other security of a company may not perform as well as expected, and may decrease in value, because of factors related to the company, to the industry in which the company is engaged, or to the market as a whole. The Fund is subject to the risk that the Adviser or Subadviser may make poor security selections. Funds that invest a greater proportion of their assets in the securities of a smaller number of issuers will be subject to greater volatility with respect to their investments than funds that invest in a larger number of securities.

Securities of smaller companies are usually less stable in price and less liquid than those of larger, more established companies. Therefore, they generally involve greater risk.

data.title: Restaurant Brands International: A Shakier Bet As Rivals Pull Ahead (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
If there’s one thing most investors can likely agree on, it’s that the stock market in 2026 has proven to be exceptionally difficult to invest in. Amid market volatility, this is not a time for passive index investing: in my view, we should be deploying careful stock picking to buy deep bargains (many in the tech sector, after the AI-driven rout) while also culling out names from our portfolios where recent fundamentals are beginning to wane.

Restaurant Brands International (QSR), the parent company behind Burger King and Tim Hortons, is one of those companies that looks like it's on shakier footing. After stronger comp sales earlier in the year, RBI closed out Q4 with a whimper, while fast food giant McDonald's (MCD) is pulling ahead. Amid ever-increasing indication that the fast food industry is becoming more price-competitive, it's time to take caution on this stock after its recent 5%+ post-earnings pullback.

I last wrote a buy article on Restaurant Brands International in early January, when the stock was trading just shy of $70. Since then, RBI's Q4 earnings print, which showcased a slowdown in sales across all of its major brands, has made me more jittery about the impacts of the macroeconomy on dine-in traffic and the increased aggression of rivals to grab price-conscious consumers. With this in mind, I'm dropping my rating on RBI to neutral.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for RBI. On the bright side for the company:

  • International expansion surge. While comp sales in the U.S. are slowing down thanks to a more unfavorable macroeconomy, Restaurant Brands is achieving far more solid, high single-digit comps growth in international markets. It's also broadening its footprint for its concepts overseas, with the company growing the number of Tim Hortons and Popeyes locations overseas by 9% y/y and 26% y/y, respectively.
  • Margin expansion. Even in spite of comps sluggishness in the U.S./Canada markets, RBI's increasing mix of franchise sales is helping the company to drive operating margin expansion, defying ingredient inflation and labor rate increases.

At the same time, however, we have to be wary of the following red flags:

  • Competitors are getting much more aggressive on price competition. The restaurant sector has seen a hollowing out of the mid-tier “fast casual” names like Chipotle (CMG) and CAVA (CAVA), as consumers respond to price increases by visiting less frequently (as evidenced by declining comp sales and traffic metrics). Conversely, McDonald's has grabbed share by emphasizing promotions, especially its nationally advertised $5 Meal Deal. RBI is facing a much more promotional sales environment.
  • Huge debt load. RBI is shouldering a net debt load of over $12.5 billion, which represents greater than a 4x leverage ratio against trailing adjusted EBITDA. Most commercial banks typically consider 3x to be the upper threshold at which a company is highly leveraged.

Against the counterbalancing rise of RBI's risk factors, I find the stock's valuation fair, but not entirely compelling. As shown below, the stock trades at a 16x P/E ratio and a 12x forward adjusted EBITDA ratio, on the lower end relative to peers (only more expensive than Wendy's (WEN), which is struggling with top-line declines). That said, at this juncture I'd rather pay a slight premium for a more surefire bet like McDonald's than make the bet that RBI can maintain market share in its critical home markets.

My recommendation here is to reduce allocation to RBI and wait until either we see more of a green light from comps trends or until RBI's share price drops much further.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through RBI's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

RBI's Q4 revenue grew 7.4% y/y to $2.47 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $2.41 billion (+5.1% y/y) by a respectable two-point margin. The growth was fairly split between ~3% comparable sales growth as well as ~3% net restaurant growth, mostly concentrated in international markets (especially from the company's smaller concepts, which are Popeyes and Firehouse Subs), which led to 5.8% year-over-year growth in systemwide sales globally across all brands, to $12.13 billion.

Where we are paying the most attention, however, is to the company's comp sales trends (often called same-restaurant sales by other companies, and which, as a reminder, measures revenue growth from existing restaurant locations and excludes the contribution of newly opened or recently closed stores).

The chart below showcases comp trends for the company's domestic businesses. Tim Hortons, which is the company's largest brand by revenue (due to a higher proportion of company-owned stores), saw home market (Canada) growth rates cut in half sequentially, to 2.4% y/y in Q4, versus 4.8% in Q3. Burger King, which is the company's largest brand by overall system sales (and its second largest in revenue), saw comps slow to 2.6%, 60bps weaker relative to 3.2% growth in Q3. Popeyes and Firehouse Subs, which together account for just over 10% of overall company revenue in FY25, also saw minor slowdowns.

We do note that growth rates are still stronger than the first half of the year. Also, RBI has made up for domestic weakness by showcasing stronger growth in international markets. As shown below, Burger King saw stronger 5.8% y/y growth overseas (more than double its U.S. performance), while Popeyes accelerated to 12% y/y growth.

Where we are concerned, however, is the comparison versus the industry leader: McDonald's. In Q4, McDonald's delivered startling 5.7% y/y overall comps growth and 6.8% y/y growth in the U.S., which accelerated 210bps and 440bps versus 3.6% y/y overall and 2.4% y/y U.S. comps in Q3, respectively.

Now, we caution against reading too much into a single quarter's trends. But we also can't ignore the fact that that performance is diverging between Burger King and McDonald's. The latter is accelerating sharply, and the snapshot above specifically calls out both positive check increases (price/mix benefits) and traffic counts driven by successful promotions. We're impressed that promotional activity has not impacted the average check. All indications point to the fact that, at least domestically, McDonald's is taking market share.

We note that RBI still drove meaningful margin expansion. Adjusted operating income rose ~16% y/y to $674 million, reflecting a 27.3% margin that improved 2 points versus 25.1% in the year-ago quarter.

As shown in the chart below, in FY25 RBI delivered $1.45 billion of free cash flow, up 11% y/y.

And while the company has made progress in a slight deleveraging over the past year, we still note its year-end 4.2x leverage ratio skews high.

Key takeaways

Q4 has clearly demonstrated something we've always known: fast food is an incredibly competitive industry. Lately, we've seen sharper impacts of McDonald's leaning into pricing and promotions to drive traffic and market share. While Restaurant Brands remains attractively priced, its appeal is waning as domestic comps slow. For now, maintain caution and move to the sidelines.

data.title: Gold Has A Path Higher, But You Need To Account For Swings (In Both Directions)
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the macro backdrop with a focus on gold and why I still believe in it for 2026. My followers know this has been a thematic idea of mine for quite some time, and, given how volatile the space has been in the last few weeks, I figured it was time for an update on the metal. Importantly, despite the ebbs and flows of late, gold has delivered a strong return since my last bullish review:

It is clear that it has been a good time to be an investor in gold! But after giving ourselves a pat on the back, we have to now consider if it will still be a good time to be an investor in gold for the coming weeks and months. There are times it can be important to "capture" wins when they come up and move on to other opportunities - but that is not the message I would relay here.

I believe gold has more room to run, but I would caution readers to be able to withstand heightened volatility in the process. This is something many investors in the yellow metal may not be used to, and it could unnerve some. For this reason, I would emphasize knowing your own comfort level for risk and volatility and preparing accordingly. If you can handle the swings, I believe owners of gold will continue to be rewarded. I will discuss why in detail below.

Even With Swings, Gold's Run Is Historically Strong

To begin, I think it is important to put gold's performance in 2026 in perspective. Looking at historical trends, we can see this has actually been an incredible start for gold. This may seem a bit of a surprise, given it had one of its worst intraday drops in decades recently. Just two weeks ago, the headlines did not look too pretty:

But the good news is this came on the back of one of the best Januarys for gold (and silver) in history. In fact, even with that "worst day" plunge, gold still had a strong YTD return. It sat around 10% for the year when February kicked off, and that dwarfs most January returns for the last three decades:

I'm not saying this to suggest epic drops in metals don't "matter". Quite the contrary - as the title of my article here suggests. But what I am saying is perspective is always needed. Did gold (and other assets) see a wild sell-off? Yes, they did - and the reasons behind this need to be considered before buying in. But did the drop signify the end to the bull run for gold? Not in the slightest, as the metal has still seen a strong 2026 and rebounded sharply after that drop.

This suggests to me that buyers are out there and momentum can continue. So I am not going to let fear of more "down days" keep me out of this asset class. I see support for owning gold for the remainder of 2026 and when I consider that, even with sharp drops, gold still has strong returns for the year, it improves my comfort level around staying long.

Expectations Are High For Equities

A classic theme for why I initially started my journey with gold a few years ago has to do with using it as a hedge against potential equity volatility. I have viewed metals - and particularly gold - as a defensive hedge. It can be used to benefit from a weakening macro-environment, risk-off sentiment, and when interest rates are on the decline. These are all important catalysts for gold, and that story has not changed. I continue to view it as a way to protect my other holdings if we see a meaningful correction in the economy or equity markets.

So, why do I think this is still relevant today? Part of the reason is the volatility in metals didn't happen in a vacuum. Equities and other assets - like Bitcoin and non-US stocks - have also seen some above-average movement. Of course, this is a natural part of investing, and I'm not saying it is "bad". But the backdrop for equities is challenging because expectations are quite high.

We saw more than a few companies over the past few weeks get hammered post-earnings even with fairly solid numbers. But going into the year, investors had anticipated double-digit growth for the S&P 500 that exceeded 2025's performance. This is weighing on my mind because if corporate performance doesn't meet these expectations going forward, stocks will drift lower:

The bottom line here is not that equities are too risky right now. On the contrary, I own quite a few of them! But it is an environment where I see a continued need to diversify. Expectations are high, volatility is rising, and companies missing earnings estimates are being punished. This is ripe for gold buying, in my view, throughout the year as long as investors anticipate strong earnings growth for US large-caps. The reason is, if that doesn't come to pass, look out below. Gold could provide some relative safety in that case.

Global Central Bank Buying Continues

Perhaps the primary reason I got behind the gold bug trend has to do with central bank buying. This is a very powerful force - and one that has continued to benefit retail investors who have followed the trend over time. The reason is two-fold. One, central banks have a heck of a lot of buying power! If they are planning on buying more, that is going to move the market. I want to front-run that.

Two, central banks (and other government entities) are often price agnostic. What I mean by that is they are not buying in order to turn a quick profit or even a profit at all. They are likely accumulating gold for other reasons. This could include hedging away from the US dollar, building up capital reserves, or protecting their assets from sanctions. These are all valid reasons and aren't really focused on trading for short-term profits.

So what I mean by "agnostic" with regard to price is that these are entities that are going to keep on buying for geopolitical and economic motives that won't stop simply because gold has been rising in price. They don't "feel" losses the way a retail trader would! And we have seen this play out as central bank buying has remained robust despite gold's rapid price appreciation in the past year:

For me, the takeaway is very straightforward. Central banks are buying gold, and I continue to want to ride that wave. This is not going away, and these are powerful institutions that can move markets. Why bet against that? It seems like a simple way to profit from a telegraphed and widely understood thematic idea that has been reshaping global finance. I'm very content to participate in this movement, and I hope my followers give it some serious thought.

What Are Some Risks? A Strong Labor Market Is One

I have laid out that I continue to view gold positively due to recent momentum, as a hedge against equity and geopolitical uncertainty, and because central banks continue to be big buyers of the metal. All good news, right? Well, those are good attributes, but that doesn't mean there isn't any risk here!

Chief among the risks is the interest rate environment in the US. As rates have declined in the US over 2025, that supercharged gold's return. As a non-income producing asset, gold has long had an inverse relationship with rates. However, the outlook for further rate cuts is less clear in 2026 as some are predicting the new Fed Chairman (possibly Kevin Warsh) will be a bit more hawkish with respect to rates. (I personally believe he will be more dovish than anticipated, but it is a risk that he won't be).

Supporting the view that rates may not have much room to move is the US labor market. Despite declining government payrolls, US private sector hirings have been strong enough to reduce unemployment and demonstrate a general level of health:

To be clear, it is not the strength of the labor market inherently that is "bad" for gold. The reason why I see it as relevant is that as employment remains strong in the US, so too will inflation. By extension, this will limit the Federal Reserve's willingness to cut interest rates further. If the Fed does keep rate cuts on pause - or even increase rates - that will be a big headwind for gold, all other things being equal. This is something to keep in mind before determining how much you want to allocate to gold going forward.

Another Risk - Can You Handle Wild Swings?

Another risk is that the short-term trading story will be volatile. This is likely to be the case, as gold's rapid rise has changed the metal's behavior on a day-to-day basis. What was once thought of as a "steady eddie" investment has turned into a trading "meme" that is seeing much larger swings in both directions in sometimes quick succession.

Let's use this past week as an example. On February 12th, the broader market was quite calm in the morning. Gold itself hovered between a slight gain and a slight loss, and then - boom. A dramatic sell-off occurred in line with a tech drawdown that saw gold lose 3-4% in a matter of minutes:

The takeaway for me is not that this is a good or bad thing. Personally, I don't view it positively, but that is besides the point. The point is that I see swings like this continuing in the coming weeks and months. While I anticipate a higher price for gold in the future, the path to get there will be volatile. You need to be honest with yourself if this is something you can handle before having this exposure. Many investors "think" they can handle volatility, but then when it happens, it turns out they can't.

The conclusion I draw is to make sure you can find an allocation you are comfortable with and stick to it. Perhaps look to add on those days when the market sells off. If you can do that, you'll be in great shape to find "alpha" here. But if these swings will make you panic or lose sleep and then potentially sell at the wrong time, then this is probably not a good time to be entering the sector.

Bottom-Line

Gold has been one of my top-performing assets since 2025, and this has encouraged me to increase my allocation of late. Rather than let the volatility concern me, I am using those big down days as a chance to buy - and that has proved fruitful in 2026, as the year-to-date returns have been positive.

Looking ahead, I think political and trade uncertainty, as well as a global shift away from the USD will continue to push gold higher. When I couple this with resilient central bank buying (some might say hoarding), it seems like a no-brainer asset to have exposure to. However, I would urge my followers to consider the risks to the downside here and make sure they can handle large swings because that is another one of my predictions - gold will move higher but not in a straight line!

In summary, if one is underweight, this asset, I believe, still has time to get in. The big moves could actually make for good entry points in the weeks ahead, and I would suggest that my followers give this idea consideration at this time.

data.title: Dividend Growth Bi-Weekly Chat 02/16/2026
data.description:
Welcome to the forum for Dividend Growth Investing discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new article is posted every two weeks as a space for sharing of ideas, discussing concepts, and digging deeper on DGI. All previous blogs are listed in chronological succession on the main chat page.

As promised and with your valued feedback, we are publishing a new version of the article with some changes to make it more engaging. The structure of the article will now include a response from one of you in the community regarding your thoughts on DGI.

If you’d like to share your DGI thoughts with us in future editions, you can email us at moderation@seekingalpha.com and let us know. We’ll be looking at continuing to do this moving forward.

For a reminder, you can find our moderation guidelines for this space in our profile. And please share your thoughts below to continue the discussion and learning on DGI.

More on Dividend Growth Investing:

How To Retire On $500,000 With A Near-Perfect Portfolio

Even though during the accumulation phase, we would recommend everyone to save (and invest) as much as possible, when it comes to retirement, savings/capital is only one side of the equation. The other side is expenses and the cost of living.

50% In Just 5 Stocks: Why I'm Willing To Invest Big In High-Quality

I prioritize companies with durable business models, high ROIC, pricing power, optionality, strong management, and attractive reinvestment runways over simple valuation metrics.

How I Would Invest $1 Million To Retire On Dividends

Well, if you have a huge nest egg consisting of millions of dollars, the equation becomes quite simple, as you could easily just buy a low-cost index fund and live off of a very conservative percentage rule with annual withdrawals of 1%, 2%, 3% per year, which would be funded in part by the dividends that the S&P 500 (SPY)(VOO) throws off and the rest from small withdrawals that should be sustainable for the long term. You could even be more conservative and employ an all-weather type portfolio, like Ray Dalio has suggested, to further reduce your sequence of returns risk.

Covered Call ETFs Look Perfect Until You Notice This Potential Fatal Flaw

Income investors are increasingly turning to alternative strategies. One of the most popular that has emerged in recent years is investing in covered call ETFs, which hold underlying S&P 500 or NASDAQ-style index structures and then overlay a covered call strategy on top of them with the intent of generating attractive current income while also delivering returns that are at least somewhat close in nature to those delivered by the underlying indexes.

2026 Is About Volatility And 10%+ Covered Call Yields

I see a strong case for gold (GLD), which can be accessed by deploying capital in the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) - dynamically managed OTM covered call ETF that has a physical gold ETF as its underlying.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure

Past performance does not guarantee future results. Content is provided for information purposes only and does not constitute investing advice. Any views or opinions expressed do not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank.

data.title: Ladder Capital: Don't Worry About The Dividend Shortfall
data.description:
Ladder Capital (LADR) undercovered its dividend with earnings available for distribution/EAD in the fourth quarter amid a realized loan loss. The mortgage REIT, however, otherwise reported solid results for Q4'25 and has a strong liquidity position to potentially take advantage of distressed opportunities in the commercial mortgage loan market or to make up a dividend shortfall, if need be.

While Ladder Capital underearning its dividend in Q4'25 was not great, the mortgage REIT has an overall strong historical coverage trend, and the firm's balance sheet quality is overall improving. Assuming that the $5M realized loan loss was an outlier in the fourth quarter, I believe that Ladder Capital's diversified origination platform and reliance on other non-loan segments provide sufficient diversification and downside protection. Shares are now trading at a much larger discount to book value than usual, which is a mispricing that investors may be able to exploit.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Ladder a strong buy in my last coverage back in October 2025—7% BV Discount, Covered Dividend, Loan Growth—because I liked the overall coverage trend and portfolio diversification. In the last quarter, Ladder unfortunately witnessed a deterioration in its coverage profile related to one specific commercial loan that was sold at a loss, but I believe that the REIT's long-term history of supporting its dividend is to be weighed more heavily against this backdrop.

Ladder Capital suffers loan losses

Ladder Capital missed distributable earnings expectations for its fourth quarter by $0.07 per share, mainly due to an unusual net realized loan loss related to a commercial property in Portland, Oregon. As of the end of the December quarter, Ladder Capital had a total portfolio worth $5.3B, which shows a $400M increase on a quarter-over-quarter basis. The main reason for the increase in Ladder Capital's portfolio is new originations (~$433M) in the commercial mortgage loan category in Q4'25.

As I will discuss further below, Ladder Capital's fourth-quarter earnings report was under the impression of loan issues, which resulted in a dividend shortfall. However, this does not mean that the REIT's overall balance sheet quality deteriorated; in fact, quite the opposite was true: Ladder Capital's CECL reserve—which stands for Current Expected Credit Losses and quantifies the total amount the company expects to lose over the life of its current loan portfolio. Ladder Capital's CECL reserve stood at $47M in the fourth quarter, down 10% quarter-over-quarter.

Ladder Capital generated $0.17 per share in fourth-quarter distributable earnings, after tax and after the recognition of loan losses. Adjusted for loan losses of $5.0M (or $0.04 per share), Ladder Capital reported earnings available for distribution in the amount of $0.21, which fell short of the running dividend rate by $0.02 per share.

Although Ladder Capital underearned its dividend with earnings available for distribution, the mortgage REIT has a diversified portfolio (only 42% of the portfolio related to loans, the remainder being securities, CRE equity, and cash) and a very strong liquidity position. It had $879M in available liquidity at the end of Q4'25, enough to fund any short-term dividend shortfall.

As a result, Ladder Capital's dividend coverage ratio deteriorated by a relatively large 18 PP Q/Q to 91%. Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT), a key rival in the CRE finance REIT market, had a dividend coverage ratio of 109% (+7 PP Q/Q). Blackstone Mortgage Trust has the best coverage trend in the CRE finance REIT industry group, which is the result of a 24% dividend cut in mid-2024. The rightsizing of the REIT's portfolio and aggressive loan markdowns have paid off for the REIT so far. I discussed the value proposition for BXMT in more detail here: Improving Outlook For 2026.

Starwood Property Trust (STWD) has not yet reported results for its fourth quarter, but the REIT has recently seen a rise in realized loan losses as well, leading the mortgage loan origination platform to under-earn its dividend for three quarters straight. Starwood Property's overall decline in the dividend coverage ratio is the main reason why I downgraded shares last year.

Here is the distributable earnings and coverage profile trajectory for each of the three REITs discussed in the last five quarters...
